title: Vim in the AI Era
subtitle: Why a 50-year-old editing language matters more, not less
summary: Agents write the code. You write the specs, the prompts, the skills, the docs — and sometimes still the code itself. The text that drives the agents is text someone has to edit. For now, that someone is you.

blocks.text: Vim in the AI Era

It is reasonable to ask, in 2026: why learn an editor designed for a 1976 terminal, in an era where agents in your IDE write whole features from a one-line prompt, command-line agents like `claude`, `codex`, `aider`, and `copilot` do entire refactors while you read email, and the loudest voices in the field will tell you that staring at source code is already a quaint hobby?

Let's not pretend otherwise: the future of programming probably is not people typing code. It may not even be people *reading* code. For a lot of glue code, internal tooling, and one-off scripts, the honest answer is that nobody is going to look at the source — not the diff, not the file, not ever. "Vibe coding" gets dunked on, but it is where this is heading for a meaningful slice of work. Your employer will expect agent-speed output, and the human typing characters into a buffer will be the bottleneck.
So why is this book in your hands?

Because *somebody* is still editing text. Even when the agent writes every line of code, the spec it reads, the prompt it follows, the markdown that defines the project, the skill files that shape its behavior, the system instructions, the design doc, and — if you don't trust the agent to phrase it — the commit message that explains the change, all of that is text, and most of it is text you wrote. English is the new programming language, and English files want to be edited just as badly as C files ever did.
What the Loop Actually Looks Like Now

rows: Describe intent in English (or any language you speak)
Run, test, debug — with agentic workflows

blocks.text: The volume of code authored per developer is going up. The fraction of it typed by a human is going down. The volume of *driving text* — prompts, specs, agents.md files, design docs, runbooks, READMEs the agent reads before doing anything — is exploding. If you spend any meaningful part of your day editing that text, Vim is the most precise instrument for the job.

And for the work where you *do* still touch the code — reviewing a critical patch, fixing something the agent got subtly wrong, rewriting a hot path the agent made correct-but-slow, working on the agent itself — the keystroke economics of Vim are the same as they ever were. {key:c}{key:i}{key:(} still rewrites the inside of a function call in three keystrokes. The grammar does not care whether the surrounding code was typed by you, generated by `copilot`, or hallucinated by a model that ran while you slept.
Voice, Dictation, and the Grammar Match

Parts of this book were drafted by voice. Dictation is fast — often faster than typing — and it works. The interesting thing about voice is not that you are going to dictate `if (x === 0) return -1;` — nobody is going to do that, and nobody should. You will dictate English at an agent and the agent will write the code. That is fine.

What is genuinely interesting is how cleanly Vim's grammar maps onto spoken commands. Operators, motions, counts, text objects — they already have names, because Vim already treats editing as a language. So when someone bolts a voice layer onto an editor and tries to make it sound natural, the editor that fits best is the one whose internal model was already linguistic.

Talon Voice is the most polished voice-driven editing system going. Its grammar mirrors Vim's directly — "delete inside parens" issues {key:d}{key:i}{key:(} — because both systems share a model: name the operation, name the target, execute. The match is not coincidence; it is what happens when two designs converge on the same underlying structure.

As a small proof, the author some years ago ran a Vim Golf challenge using only his voice via an experimental dictation layer called *VimSpeak*. The recording is more historical curiosity than recommendation — but it shows the modal grammar surviving translation into speech almost intact, because Vim already named the operations.

blocks.caption: Watch: VimSpeak — Vim Golf by voice

blocks.text: Why Many AI Tools Ship Vim Bindings

Cursor, Zed, VS Code, Helix, JetBrains, Aider, Neovim's own AI plugins — almost every editor and IDE in the agent era ships with Vim-key support, often by default. The honest reason is not nostalgia. It is that the audience for serious agent-assisted work overlaps heavily with the audience that already speaks Vim, and the grammar composes neatly with the operations that AI editing adds: jumping to a hunk, accepting or rejecting a suggestion, applying a patch, navigating a diff.
These are motion-and-operator gestures. They fit the same key vocabulary you already know.
An Honest Word About the Future

This book is not going to pretend that humans typing characters is the future of software. It very likely is not. The trajectory is clear: less code read by humans, less code typed by humans, more software written per unit of human effort, more decisions made at the level of intent rather than implementation. Eventually the question "which text editor do you use?" will sound the way "which assembler do you use?" sounds today.
So who is this book for?

It is for people who, *for now*, want to stay in control of the machine. People who write enough prose, code, specs, prompts, or configs that the keystroke-level cost of editing actually matters. People whose employer expects high output and who would rather not be the slow part of the pipeline. People who enjoy the craft of editing for its own sake.

Your author, when he retires, intends to spend his time on retro computing — old machines, old tools, programs that fit in your head, for the pure pleasure of it. Hobby projects deserve the slow path. Work, for the moment, does not. The point of Vim today is that it can serve both: it is the fastest editor available, *and* it is a deep enough craft to enjoy on its own terms. That combination is rare, and as long as text is still a thing humans touch, it is worth learning.

Treat Vim the way you treat touch typing: a once-in-a-career investment that compounds for as long as you still type. Whatever fraction of your day that is — and it is probably shrinking — make that fraction as fast and as pleasant as it can possibly be.
